sentences.text: Yes.
And on the 29th day of April, in the morning, we got up.
There was nobody around.
I mean, no Germans around, that we seen.
Just disappeared during the night.
So we broke out the gates, and we were going out.
While we were walking out of the camp, we saw American soldiers coming-- were lining, actually, the road on each side, with some troop carriers in the center.
Some tanks came by.
But mostly what I remember is the soldiers, the foot soldiers, who are walking, one after the other, on each side of the road.
And-- and we greeted them.
We were the welcoming committee for the American army, out of Allach.
And they knew who we are, because we were wearing these striped clothes, and they were familiar with this, I guess.
But they started-- they wanted to talk to us.
Most of us didn't speak English.
Most of them didn't speak any other language.
But there were some Jews among these-- among our-- the people, and they speak in Yiddish.
So they knew that we are Jews, and they are-- were addressing us in Yiddish, and this is how we-- we got together.
And we identified each other, and we-- actually, we-- we started to get acquainted with the liberating army.
And-- but we needed food.
And not just junk.
Because what they had on themselves was candy and chewing gum, and they wanted to give us all of this.
And-- and some of us took it and got sick from it.
I did, too.
Because I wasn't any smarter than the others.
But soon after this-- these were the-- the forward troops.
After this, about-- a couple of hours later, the-- the kitchen came.
You know, the-- the-- the-- I don't know what the term is to describe the providers who provide the army with food and all this.
And there was already.
And some medical staff came.
And some of us, of course, were really sick.
And they were trying to take care of us.
And they located-- you know, went back into the barracks.
And to make like a temporary, the best way-- temporary home, the best way we can, they to do-- tried to provide some kind of food compatible to our condition.
And this didn't take long for me, because I didn't stay.
Since this was only seven kilometers from Munich, I walked to Munich.
Not all myself.
A few other people who were able to walk.
And there, we got in touch with some authorities.
Right away, there were some Germans, and also Americans, set up some offices, some organization, to be able to take care of these refugees of-- of prisoners, you know, camp inmates and so on.
And they were setting up some kitchens and-- and some barracks.
And also, they took over some hospitals, German hospitals, and the other buildings that they could set up beds and so on for us.
But very soon, I was able-- you know, we formed a committee, right away.
And very soon, I was able to get an apartment from a German, just threw him out and got an apartment in-- in Munich itself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they have-- we have really, under the supervision of the Americans, the American Joint Distribution Committee, set up offices in Munich.
And they asked people who would want to volunteer to work for them, to do office work or whatever was necessary.
I was one of the first volunteers.
And there was also a Jewish committee.
Aside of the Joint, the American Joint, there was a Jewish committee set up of-- of-- of survivors.
So under the supervision and guidance of the Joint, we were able to set up some offices, started to register people, survivors.
And get information-- where are you from?
And who survived?
And who do you know?
And so on, trying to make-- take record of-- of-- of the survivors.
And provide them information, and-- and provide them with food.
Because then, there was nobody else to feed them.
The Germans certainly were not in position, because they [? own-- ?] at this time, they had their very meager rations.
And even though after a very short time, all the survivors received ration cards, they were able to draw rations from the same sources as the Germans did.
But we had our very good supplements from the American sources.
And I was working in this Jewish committee.
It was on [?
Milstraus ?] Street, It didn't have the street.
It was a quite nice, large building, and they-- [INAUDIBLE].
It's-- its amazing how fast we organized ourselves to set up these things.
Because it was under the guidance of the Americans, who had the experience, but here, they were amateurs who had come from all walks of life and from all kinds, all countries.
And here we started to set up something.
Some other people started to barter things with the Germans, and we created a black market.
There's no question about it, that the survivors contributed a great deal to the black market.
Maybe they were the-- the-- the founders of the black market.
Who knows?
But this was-- these were the facts of life, and the necessities of life.
And since the Jews have this knack for-- for dealing and wheeling-- we have this, I guess, inborn in us.
So it came easier to us than it would have come to other nationalities, probably.
And very soon, some of us got wealthy.
Some of us stayed with the Jewish committee, working for them, like I did.

And in-- in September of that very year, 1945, I got news from somebody who came back from-- from Krakow that my two brothers survived and my sister survived-- they were in Krakow-- and also that my wife survived.
And she came, actually, to join me in Munich in September.
They were also in-- in all kinds of camps.
Like me, first in the ghetto and then in camps.
But they was liberated by the Russians.
So they were very close to Krakow.
So as soon as they were liberated, they went back to Krakow.
It was natural for them.
I was liberated in Munich.
We knew that they are-- they were dead.
They were taken from Bochnia.
They were taken with my two sisters.
They were taken away, most probably to Belzec, and we never heard of them again.
And my wife, as I said, survived, and she came back to Krakow.
And in September, she came to Germany, to Munich.
She found out that I am alive.
And we had a reunion in September 1945.
Now, in Munich, I was four years, until October 26, 1949.
This was when I arrived here.
In Munich, we-- my daughter was born in Munich.
I have one daughter, Bernice.
Her married name is Slutzman.
She was born May 13, 1949.
And by October of the same year, we came here to the United States.
Of course, during the-- the four years in Germany, there were a lot of Zionist organizations at work, trying to get people to go to Israel, particularly the Revisionist Zionists.
And-- but I had two uncles, my brothers-- my my father's brothers, in New York.
They came here in 1912 and 1914, respectively.
And we got in touch with them after the war.
We were able to-- because I-- I-- I remembered their addresses.
Not the-- the-- the exact addresses, but I remembered they lived in the Bronx.
And somehow my letters reached them.
The name-- and-- and in the Bronx, the letters reached them.
And we got in touch, and they sent us-- one of them sent us affidavits to come to the United States.
But unfortunately, I did not meet any of those.
They both died before I came to this country.
And they were young men.
They probably died in the-- one died in his 50s.
I came to America--
In October 26, 1949.
Not ship.
By plane.
Why did I come by plane?
Because we had a daughter, five-- five-months-old daughter.
And at this time, they started to use planes also for transportation, but they gave preference for people who had very small infants.
And we belonged to this category, because she was only five months old.
And we got here-- this is another story, how we got here by plane.
We boarded the plane in Munich.
And there was a stopover in Shannon, in Ireland.
And then the next stopover was in Greenland.
It took us 30 hours to come from Munich to the United States.
It was a propeller train-- a plane.
And in Greenland, we-- there was a stopover of a few hours, and there was a-- no-- a blizzard.
It was in October, but there was a blizzard in Greenland.
And we had to dis-- de-embark-- de-embark?-- the plane, and go over to some, you know, to-- like on the airport.
Because either they changed place or whatever, they couldn't keep us in the place for hours.
And I remember carrying the baby, walking through that deep snow, from the plane.

Because they were-- the facilities were not like they have it now, the modern facilities, you go directly from the-- from the plane to the-- to the-- not to the airport, to the-- to the facilities there.
So we were walking through the snow, heavy snow.
And we waited there, until they announced that the next plane is ready.
And they took us, brought us here.
The whole voyage, the whole trip, took us 30 hours.
Arriving here at the airport, they took us to Ellis Island from the airport.
At Ellis Island, I was met by my aunt, who survived this uncle who sent me the affidavit, and my cousins, which is his-- her-- her sons-- son.
And my sister was here already before.
She-- she came three months earlier than I did.
They're waiting, and my brother also came before me.
So they were all waiting for me at Ellis Island-- for us, actually.
And we brought us to-- to the apartment where my aunt lived, in the Bronx.
She was a widow.
And actually, she wanted to go to Israel after the death of her husband, my uncle.
But she decided since he sent us affidavits, she decided to stay and to keep the apartment for us, that we can have it when we come.
So it was a very noble deed of-- on her part.
And so we came to her apartment.
It was a walk-up, a fifth-floor walk-up, in the Bronx, but a large apartment, five rooms.
And we were happy to be here.

Well, I had very good recommendations from the American Joint Distribution Committee, because I worked as a-- as a bookkeeper, first in the Jewish committee, and then I worked in the offices of the Jewish Joint Distribution Committee.
And I learned-- I knew-- I was a little bit familiar with the English language.
I took some lessons before the war.
But-- and then working under American supervision, I learned the language much better.
And I came here equipped with recommendations from-- from them, you know, as a bookkeeper, it's also-- I was already typing and writing letters, and you know.
So when I came here to the Jewish Joint Distribution Committee, and I showed them these papers, they said, we cannot give you a job.
You're overqualified.
So I couldn't get a job in any profession, so I just went to a factory.
And I worked with pocketbooks In fact, I became a framer of pocketbooks.
You know, the old-type pocketbooks had frames, the way you close it.
And the frames had to be covered with the plastic or with the leather that the pocketbook was made of.
So this was a special art, to frame pocketbooks.
So I became a framer of pocketbooks.
I make $0.75 an hour.
And-- and I came home with bloody fingers, because this is-- you know, to-- to fit a plastic, hard plastic, on that frame, was-- it was a hard job.
But this was a job.
I made $29 a week after-- after deductions, because they deducted Social Security and something, they deducted from me.
And?
And then after several weeks, I couldn't take this job.
First of all, I didn't make enough money to pay the rent and to sustain us.
So I got another job.
And in 1960-- and I worked in the shop for 10 years.
And I made a decent living.
In 1960, we decided to go into business, and I bought, with the help of some loans from relatives, I bought a dry-cleaning store.
And I was in this business until 1980, when I retired.
Presently, yes.
I have two grandchildren.
It's Mary Ruth and Jonathan Evan.
Is Stewart.
That was 1984.
I remarried, yeah, 1985.
Is Helena.
It's pronounced-- I pronounce it Helena, the way they call her.
Because here, you would say Helene.
Her maiden name is [?
Roitman. ?]
She was in-- in Russia during the war.
She also-- you would say she was a survivor because she ran away from the Germans, and she spent three years in-- under very hard conditions in Russia.
Yes, not only presently, since 1965.
People who come-- originate from Krakow have created a society.
It's called the New Cracow Friendship Society.
I became active in this society.
Momentarily, I was elected to the board of directors.
And then I served as president of the society for six years, from 1983 to 1989.
But all through that time, since almost 1967, two years after the establishment of the organization, I became the editor of the-- of our newsletter that we publish.
At the beginning, it was a monthly.
Now it's every two months.
I have here a copy of this.
The organization, well, as the name would imply, it was created for a purpose that we should get together in-- as a substitute for families that we have lost.
Be together in friendship, organize functions that suit our purpose.
But very soon, we found out that this type of organization doesn't have a future.
Just friendship doesn't have a future.
We have to be attached to some kind of compassion, some charity, to something that would do-- that you would do for others, rather than for yourself.
Because this is a self-serving-- would be self-- too much self-serving to really be able to be sustained.
So we have-- in this organization, we have organized all kinds of committees in charge of charities.
You established a welfare fund, which is helping Jews that still live today in Krakow and Jews of Krakow descent that live in Israel.
So this welfare fund exists already now for over 27 years.
And we are sending money to a lot of Jews in Krakow and in Israel every month.
How many people are in this organization?
The organization now has a little less than 400 family members.
But besides that, we attached ourselves to the causes of Israel.
We are supporting many causes, like Israel bonds.
We have a function every year and we try to sell Israel bonds.
Some years, we are selling bonds for over $1 million, by our organization alone, without the help of outsiders.
Then we also adopted organizations in Israel, like Akim.
Akim is an organization for the rehabilitation of handicapped children in Israel.
And in 1975, this was a completely new organization, Akim.
And we got wind of it, and particularly our women got very enthusiastic about the idea of helping handicapped children.
So we formed a special committee that would raise funds to help Akim.
Now we have an establishment in Hadera [DRINKS] that was built by our funds, and we keep supporting it ever since.
Besides that, we have attached ourself-- attached ourselves to an organization that is called Beit Halochem which is the House of the Warriors.
This is the translation of Beit Halochem.
This is an organization to rehabilitate Jewish soldiers who were wounded or in any way are handicapped, you know, in the Israeli wars.
And the-- the purpose of that organization, Beit Halochem, is to help rehabilitate these-- these soldiers and bring them back to become regular, productive citizens, if possible.
Some of them will never be, because they are-- they lost some limbs.
Some lost their eyesight.
But they are all being helped and rehabilitated in these facilities.
In fact, we were in Israel three weeks ago, and we visited a new facility in Jerusalem of Beit Halochem.
And they showed us the wonders that they do there for-- to rehabilitate the-- the-- the invalids.
Besides-- well, besides that, we are still a friendship organization, and we do have get-togethers on certain occasions.
Like, we organize Hanukkah parties.
We organize Yom Ha'atzmaut parties.
This is when Israel celebrates the independence.
And we get together-- in fact, next Sunday, we have a Yom Ha'atzmaut party.
It's delayed now, because a lot of people were in Israel three weeks ago, and we have celebrated the Yom Ha'atzmaut in Israel this time.
So we have a lot of our-- our society is very diversified.
And it's very-- it's not only diversified, it is being acknowledged among-- in the survivor community.

I, personally, also work with Benjamin Meed, who is the chairman of the-- of the WAGRO-- this is the Warsaw ghetto uprising organization-- and also the Together Federation of Jewish-- of Jewish Survivors.
And occasionally, I go to meetings that he has, and he calls, or he sends me notices and invitations.
And we work with Yad Vashem, contribute occasionally to the publications that Yad Vachem has.

And their publication is And the publication of Benjamin Meed is called Besides that, though I am quite busy publishing our own newsletter, which I will want to show you at least the cover of it, of our last issue.
And it gives me a lot of satisfaction, and it gives me something to do in my retirement.
Well, this is a standard question that every survivor hears at one time or another.
If I was very religious and believing individual, I would say there was a purpose.
I survived with a mission, to carry on the memory.
And I mean, this is a point of view.
But I-- I am not a hypocrite.
And to say that I survived so that I can carry the message is a little-- to-- to me, it's far-fetched.
It doesn't mean that it doesn't-- it doesn't put an obligation on me to do just that.
This is the reason that I am doing this interview, that people, other people, can learn from it, and-- and-- and the people who try to deny it will be refuted by this-- by this-- by my testimony.
But if you asked me why I think I survived, only God knows.
It was lots of luck, lots of luck, and lots of luck.
I have dreams.
I always had dreams.
They're not-- maybe not as frequent as some other people's.
Some-- there are people who really suffer from these dreams almost every night.
I don't get them every night.
But occasionally, but occasionally, I get things-- to see things that I forgot completely that they happened.
And all of a sudden, they come alive.
Well, most of the dreams are that I lived in fear.
And that awesome, awesome fear of the Germans.
You know, it's-- it is-- you cannot really-- I cannot express this in-- in words to explain what that fear means.
And I think people who didn't go through it, who didn't live through it, cannot imagine what that means.
Because it is an apathy combined with fear.
On the one hand, I don't care, because I lost everybody already.
What-- what does it-- what do I care what will happen to me?
And-- and on the other hand, it's this powerful fear-- what will happen to me?
It's contradictory.
I also wanted to mention, in my activities now, I'm very much involved with the Jewish Center, of which I'm not only a member, but I'm also a neighbor.
It's the-- called the Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jewish Community Center of West Hempstead.
And I also-- I-- I am very much involved in the-- in-- in our program for remembering the Shoah.
Every year, around Yom HaShoah, which is the 27th day of Nisan, a few days, five days after Pesach, most of the congregations have some programs to remember the Shoah.
I and Norbert Friedman are very-- are the chairpersons in that function of remembering the Shoah.
And we're preparing, every year, a different kind of a program to interest not only the members of the congregation, but also the community.
We invite the clergy to come, and they do come.
We invite, sometimes, politicians to come.
So we are very much involved.
When time comes, around April time, Norbert and I are very much involved in preparing programs.
And we try to be imaginative, not repetitious.
And in-- in our experience, it would-- that we have-- we had a year ago, we had on-- on our program.
And we invited one of the survivors of This time we had liberators on the program, and it was very successful.
